draft.introduction: Solon’s surviving poems and fragments articulate his political philosophy as Athens’s reformer-legislator (594 BCE). They defend the middle path between oligarchic greed and popular excess, establishing justice (eunomia) as the foundation of the city.

translation.text: Our city will never perish by the decree of Zeus or the will of the blessed gods; but the citizens themselves, persuaded by wealth, wish to destroy it by their foolishness. Good order (eunomia) makes all things well-ordered and fitting, and often puts shackles on the unjust. She smoothes what is rough, stops excess, and dims arrogance.
translation.original_text: ἡμετέρη δὲ πόλις κατὰ μὲν Διὸς οὔποτ’ ὀλεῖται

translator_notes.note: Solon’s eunomia (good order) became a foundational concept in Greek political thought, contrasted with dysnomia (lawlessness). His insistence that the city’s destruction comes from human greed, not divine will, represents a crucial secularization of political analysis.
